content.text: You Might Not Need an Effect
useEffect

is powerful, but it is frequently used where it is not needed. Overusing effects introduces extra re-renders, complex timing, and harder-to-read code. The React team's guidance is direct: if you can express the same logic without an effect, you should. This sub-subtopic enumerates the most common cases where effects are added unnecessarily — and the simpler pattern that replaces them.

attrs.alt: Decision tree: should this be an effect?

content.text: Before examining the anti-patterns, it helps to state clearly what
useEffect
is actually for:
synchronising a component with an external system.
External systems include the browser DOM, network APIs, timers, WebSockets, and third-party libraries. If there is no external system involved, there is usually no need for an effect.
The rule of thumb:
if no external system is involved, the logic probably belongs either in the render body, in
useMemo
, or in an event handler.
Anti-pattern 1: Derived State in an Effect
The most common unnecessary effect: computing a value from props or state and storing the result in state. This adds an extra render (the one triggered by the effect's
setState
call) and unnecessary complexity.
The computed value updates synchronously during the render in which
props.items
changes. No extra re-render is needed and there is no timing gap where the derived value is stale.
Anti-pattern 2: Event Logic in an Effect

Logic that runs in response to a user action belongs in the event handler, not in an effect. An effect that watches state changed by a user event is an indirect path — put the logic directly in the handler.
The logic is now co-located with the event, runs exactly once, and does not depend on React's effect scheduling. It is also easier to test in isolation.
Anti-pattern 3: Notifying a Parent in an Effect

Notifying a parent when something changes is a common pattern. An effect that calls a callback prop when state changes is roundabout; the event handler that updates the state is the right place to call the callback.
Anti-pattern 4: Resetting State on Prop Change
Resetting child state when a prop changes is often done with an effect that calls
setState
when a key prop changes. The idiomatic React way is to use the component's
key
prop to force a full remount, or to move the state up.
key prop remount:
When
key
changes, React unmounts the component and mounts a fresh instance. All state resets to initial values. This is idiomatic React for isolating instances.
Anti-pattern 5: Transforming Data for Display
Transforming data for display — sorting, filtering, grouping — does not require an effect or even
useMemo
unless the computation is genuinely expensive (benchmarked). Compute it inline.
useMemo
is the right tool when a computation is expensive enough to skip on unchanged inputs. "Expensive" means measured — a simple sort over a few hundred items is fast enough to do inline.
Anti-pattern 6: Initialising State from a Prop
Initialising state from a prop or external value is done via the
useState
initialiser — a function passed to
useState
that runs exactly once on mount. An effect that sets initial state triggers an extra re-render for no reason.
The initialiser function runs only on the first render.
useState(() => computeInitial())
is always preferable to
useState(computeInitial())
when the computation has a cost, but for reading a prop as initial state, the direct form is fine.
Anti-pattern 7: Chained Effects (Transform After Fetch)

Transforming fetched data in a second effect (first effect fetches, second effect transforms when the raw data changes) adds an extra render and indirection. Transform the data in the same effect before calling
setState
When an Effect IS the Right Tool
After removing all unnecessary effects, the remaining uses of
useEffect
should all have an external system on the other side:
Use case
Why effect is correct
fetch / network request
External system (server); must run after render
addEventListener
External DOM; must clean up
setInterval / setTimeout
Browser timer API; must cancel on cleanup
WebSocket
Long-lived connection; must close on cleanup
Third-party widget init (Leaflet, Chart.js)
External library; needs DOM to exist
document.title update
Directly writes to browser API outside React tree
Anti-pattern 8: Reading localStorage in an Effect
localStorage
reads are synchronous and do not involve an external event stream — there is nothing to subscribe to. Reading from
localStorage
during render or as a
useState
initialiser is perfectly safe and does not need an effect.
Writing to
localStorage
after state changes is one case where an effect is appropriate — it is a synchronisation with the browser storage API. But reading the initial value is always done in the initialiser, not an effect.
Anti-pattern 9: Reading URL Params in an Effect
Reading URL search parameters to initialise a filter or page value does not require an effect. The router provides the current URL synchronously; read it in the render or in the state initialiser.
An effect that reads
window.location
and sets state is redundant when the router already exposes the current URL as a synchronous value. Use the router's hook directly.
Anti-pattern 10: Deriving Values from Context in an Effect
Values from context are synchronously available during render. Computing derived values from context does not require an effect.
Anti-pattern 11: Validation in an Effect

Form validation that runs when input values change is often placed in an effect, but computed validation is simpler: derive the error messages inline from the current field values. No effect, no extra render.
Cost of Unnecessary Effects
Every unnecessary effect adds overhead:
Each setState call in an effect triggers an extra render cycle — often doubling render count per state change.
The UI may flicker or show stale intermediate state between the first render and the effect's state update.
Cleanup and effect timing add complexity that makes the component harder to reason about.
Strict Mode double-invokes effects in development, amplifying the extra renders.
Code that expresses intent directly (during render) is easier to trace in DevTools than deferred effect logic.
Refactoring an Unnecessary Effect
A practical refactoring approach when you suspect an effect is unnecessary:
Identify: does the effect interact with any external system? If not, it may be unnecessary.
Check: is the effect triggered by a user event? Move the logic to the handler.
Check: is the effect computing a value from existing state/props? Move it to render or useMemo.
Check: is the effect setting initial state? Use the useState initialiser instead.
Check: is the effect transforming data after a fetch? Transform inside the fetch .then() callback.
If the effect remains after all checks, it is likely legitimate — leave it.
Guiding question:
"Which external system is this effect keeping in sync?" If you cannot name one, the logic probably belongs in render,
useMemo
, or an event handler.
Summary
Effects exist to synchronise components with external systems, not to compute values or respond to events within React. Most over-use of
useEffect
falls into a small set of patterns — each has a simpler replacement.
Derived state:
compute during render; useMemo for expensive cases.
Event logic:
put it in the event handler, not an effect watching the resulting state.
Parent notification:
call the callback prop directly in the handler that updates state.
State reset on prop change:
use the key prop to remount; let React reset state.
Data transformation:
filter/sort inline or in useMemo; no effect or extra state.
Initial value from prop:
useState(initialValue) — one argument, no effect.
Chained effects:
consolidate into one effect; transform before calling setState.
